moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with this flexible knight move, a hallmark of the King's Indian Attack system. By developing Nf3 before committing our center pawns, we keep maximum flexibility—we can later support e4 or d4 depending on Black's setup. This move controls the central squares e5 and d4 while preparing a kingside fianchetto, giving us a characteristic setup that's both solid and dynamically balanced.

Black mirrors our development with excellent piece coordination. By playing Nf6, Black controls the e4 and d5 squares while preparing to support a kingside fianchetto or central advance. This flexible response doesn't commit Black to any particular plan yet, which is why strong players often prefer it in positions where White has avoided early central commitments.

We commit to the King's Indian Attack setup, preparing to fianchetto our kingside bishop on g2. This pawn move supports long-term control of the long diagonal (a1-h8) and creates a solid structure around our king. The fianchetto system allows us to maintain flexibility in the center while building pressure along the long diagonal and potentially on the queenside.

Black follows suit with their own fianchetto preparation, creating a balanced, symmetrical pawn structure in the kingside. By playing g6, Black prepares to place their bishop on g7, where it will exert strong pressure along the long diagonal and support potential central breaks. This setup gives Black a solid, flexible position that can handle various White plans.

This is the key move of the Smyslov Variation—we launch a queenside expansion before completing our setup! By playing b4, we seize space and prepare b5 to gain more room and potentially disrupt Black's queenside coordination. This aggressive approach is what distinguishes Smyslov's treatment: rather than playing a slow positional game, we create immediate practical problems by threatening to expand further with b5, forcing Black to decide how to respond to our queenside ambitions.
